claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let me ask you a few questions about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

1. **How old is the child** you want to claim Child Benefit for? (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and we can check each of them)

2. **What is the child's name?** (This will help me keep track if we're discussing multiple children)

The age of the child is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please share these details and we'll work through your eligibility together!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to ask about.

My eldest is Alex, who's 6 years old, and my youngest is Blake, who's 3.

Should we go through them one at a time, or can we check both together?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you check eligibility for both Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 3).

We can definitely assess both children, and I'll work through the questions for each of them. Since both Alex and Blake are **under 16 years old**, they're in the automatic qualification age category, which is a good start!

Let me continue with the assessment. For children under 16, the next important check is about their work status.

**For Alex (age 6):**
Is Alex currently in any paid work or employment for 24 hours or more per week?

(I realize at age 6 this is very unlikely, but I need to ask this as part of the official assessment process!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Ha, no, Alex is only 6! They're in primary school, not working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's what I expected – Alex is definitely not working full-time at age 6!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, the next question for **Alex**:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right** – such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Again, at age 6, this is very unlikely, but it's part of the official checks!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just 6, so nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's what I expected. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now for **Alex**, one more question about their status:

**Is Alex currently in any type of apprenticeship?**

(Again, at age 6 this doesn't apply, but I need to check this for the assessment!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not! Alex is only 6 and in primary school, so no apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is clearly not in an apprenticeship. Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we're moving on to questions about **you as the claimant**. These questions will apply to both Alex and Blake.

**Residency Requirements:**

Do you meet any of the following residency requirements? (You only need to meet ONE of these):

1. **Do you normally live in the UK?**
2. Do you have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?
3. Do you have pre-settled status and are working/seeking work/studying?
4. Are you a Crown servant living abroad?

The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK. **Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, regarding your responsibility for Alex and Blake:

**Do you live with Alex and Blake?** 

Or if you don't live with them, do you contribute to their upkeep (paying for their food, clothes, housing, etc., at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since both Alex and Blake live with you, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Anyone else

If someone else is claiming, have you agreed with them that you should be the one to claim instead?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes things straightforward. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check the living situation for both children:

**Are Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, Alex is living normally at home with me. 

But Blake... Blake has been in hospital for a few months now. They've been there for quite a while - it's been really difficult.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm so sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital for an extended period. That must be very difficult for your family.

Let me check the rules for Blake's situation, as there are specific time limits for children in hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. The rules state that if a child has been in hospital for **more than 12 weeks** continuously, it affects Child Benefit eligibility. However, if there have been breaks of less than 28 days between hospital stays, those periods are "linked" together.

**Can you tell me:**

1. **How many weeks has Blake been in hospital continuously?** (Or approximately how many months?)

2. **Has Blake had any breaks at home during this time?** If so, were they longer or shorter than 28 days?

This will help me determine Blake's eligibility accurately.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake has been in hospital for about 3 months now - so that's around 13 weeks I think. It's been continuous, no breaks where they've come home at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I'm very sorry you're going through this.

Unfortunately, because Blake has been in hospital continuously for approximately 13 weeks, this exceeds the 12-week limit set by the Child Benefit rules. This means that Child Benefit would not be payable for Blake during this extended hospital stay.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, let me complete the assessment for **Alex**, who is living normally at home:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(This would typically apply if the council was covering costs for Alex's care or living arrangements)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Alex. They just live at home with me and I take care of everything.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's great news for Alex's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me provide you with a complete assessment for both of your children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Summary**

I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here's what I found:

---

### **✅ ALEX (Age 6) - ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the eligibility requirements:
- ✓ Under 16 years old
- ✓ Not in full-time work
- ✓ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✓ Not in an apprenticeship
- ✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✓ You live with Alex and are responsible for their care
- ✓ No one else is claiming for Alex
- ✓ Alex lives normally at home with you
- ✓ The local council is not paying for Alex's care

---

### **❌ BLAKE (Age 3) - NOT ELIGIBLE (Currently)**

I'm very sorry, but Blake is not eligible for Child Benefit at this time due to their extended hospital stay. Here's why:

- Blake has been in hospital continuously for approximately 13 weeks (3 months)
- The Child Benefit rules state that benefit is **not payable if a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks continuously**
- Blake has exceeded this 12-week limit

**Important:** Once Blake comes home from hospital, you can reclaim Child Benefit for them. The eligibility can be reassessed at that time.

---

### **Next Steps for Alex:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. **Documents needed:**
   - Your proof of identity
   - Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate
3. **Be aware:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply (this is a tax charge, not an eligibility issue)
4. **Payments:** You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

I hope Blake recovers soon and can come home. When that happens, please get in touch with HMRC to restart Child Benefit for Blake. If you have any other questions, I'm here to help!

reasonings: Alex (age 6) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all eligibility criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in an apprenticeship; (5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK (Manchester); (6) You live with Alex and are responsible for them; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home with you; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All criteria are satisfied.

Blake (age 3) is INELIGIBLE for Child Benefit due to extended hospital stay. While Blake meets the basic age qualification (under 16) and other personal criteria, Blake has been in hospital continuously for approximately 13 weeks (about 3 months). The Child Benefit rules state that if a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks continuously, Child Benefit is not payable during that extended hospital stay. Blake exceeds this 12-week limit, making them ineligible while they remain hospitalized beyond this period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all personal eligibility requirements (UK residency in Manchester, lives with both children, responsible for their care, no other claimants). Alex qualifies for Child Benefit as they meet all criteria. However, Blake is currently ineligible due to being hospitalized for approximately 13 weeks continuously, which exceeds the 12-week hospital stay limit. If Blake returns home, eligibility can be reassessed.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)
